data.title: Blackstone: Valuation Compression Masks Intact Earnings Power
data.description:
Blackstone Inc. (BX) looked like a Buy to me last May. Alternatives were structurally gaining share and continue to be a relevant theme today as macro uncertainties have risen considerably (from the tariff uncertainties in May 2025) and equity markets have cooled. I expected Blackstone to fully strengthen its Fee Related Earnings (FRE), leveraging its unmatched scale and distribution.

And when we look at operating data since then, almost every box does look ticked. FRE has grown, AUM has scaled, and Distributable Earnings (DE) have also increased strongly. Despite the strength, the stock is down ~15% from the time of my Buy thesis (total returns including reinvested dividends). It did initially rally by 35-40%, but the retracement from the top is even sharper than the net-net correction from the time of my May thesis.

The corrections are definitely a sharp valuation compression rather than a drop in fundamentals, which means markets are viewing earnings differently today. And the bulk of the compression has happened in 2026, keeping with wider market cooling, risk-off sentiments, and increasing macro fears, especially stagflation. That pressures the realized earnings timing visibility for Blackstone.

This analysis walks through the valuation compression and reasons and concludes that while the performance-linked earnings (or carry) could be temporarily weak (as exit uncertainties increase in a pressured macro scenario), they will normalize over time. Which means the valuation compression is an opportunity to accumulate. Because the gap between earnings reality and valuation has widened in comparison to May last year, Blackstone becomes a higher-conviction Buy in my view.

Core Engine Is Compounding

FY2025 FRE was reported as ~$5.7b, up 9% YoY, driven by ~11% growth in fee-paying AUM and ~$239b of inflows. The quality of earnings also looks good, with a growing share of the capital tagged long duration or perpetual. Perpetual capital is now 48% of the fee-earning AUM, and that is up by 18% YoY.

There is a broader mix shift in the works, with credit and insurance gaining share. Credit and insurance, along with private equity, registered the strongest gains through the year (both ~18% up YoY), but credit and insurance had a larger base, implying a higher share of the AUM increase. This should mean more stable and recurring fees, although immediate carry could be lower. That should balance out near-term volatility concerns by introducing more certainty for the base earnings. The trade-off could be that carry could be slightly deferred, although not diminished.

DE was also up in 2025, with DE up 19% (~$7.1b), indicating cash conversion mechanics remain strong. DE growing faster than FRE indicates a strong monetization environment - in fact net realizations were up ~50% for the full year.

Q4 FRE did see some drop, but that seems to be a function of a mechanical distortion rather than any signal of underlying weakness. Excluding the impact of $1b recognized in Q4 2024 from infrastructure crystallizations, FRE would have actually shown strong YoY growth (~24%).

The Carry Environment

The carry debate seems to be critical in explaining the valuation compression, so let us look deeper into what is already happening versus what is potentially being feared. The monetization environment is not weak currently. We have already charted the sharp spike in net realizations, especially in Q4.

On the other hand, Net Accrued Performance Revenues (NAPR), which is an indicator of carry earned but not realized, has moved up from ~$6.3b to ~$6.7b through 2025. So, the questions are not around value creation, but rather the potentially uneven timing for realizations. Looking back, uneven timing is a given in the business, with the lumpier realization seen in Q4. Management commentary in the Q4 earnings call indicates a reopening of capital markets and what was being seen as early stages of normalization. But we are still in a regime where consistent deal activity is not happening. Since realizations remain subject to the macro conditions, a worsening macro scenario (higher for longer rates in a stagflation scenario, risk-off sentiment, and uneven capital markets) becomes a cause for concern ahead, even if the 2025 story looks about alright.

There is no question around value creation in my view, as carry is still being created and accumulated. The Q4 spike in realizations does show that whenever windows open, exits and realizations can accelerate sharply, but the uncertainty around timing and lumpiness is real. That shifts the debate from value to visibility (delaying earnings versus slowing them).

In fact, the reason I remain on the Buy side despite the visibility issue is that value creation is still intact. Plus, the very macro situation that raises slower exit cycle fears also means a stronger deployment phase. Higher rates and tighter liquidity could mean opportunity for stronger deal terms and attractive entry valuations for new deployments, especially in credit and private markets. Blackstone has already deployed ~$138b in 2025 in what could be higher-return vintages. According to commentary, we can already see selective deployment opportunities showing up -

We also took advantage of volatility in markets to sign or close 8 privatizations during the year in private equity and real estate, including in the fourth quarter, medical technology company, Hologic, for $18 billion.

Valuations And Opportunity

Over the past one year, the stock is down by ~24%. Depending on the cutoff periods (because the latest FRE numbers are as of December 31, 2025), we will still see the share prices are mostly down at a time when FRE has gone up by ~9%. That points to the degree of valuation compression in FRE multiple terms over 2025 and the early months of 2026.

What has potentially changed is the way markets were pricing in stable fees and performance earnings earlier, expecting carry to normalize over time. Now, the view is potentially heavy discounting applied to the carry profile (so a better FRE commands a lower multiple overall). Therein lies an opportunity to accumulate more at current valuations. What markets are penalizing is an uncertain exit environment ahead and monetization visibility rather than magnitude.

While the monetization efficiency was still seen in Q4 2025, it was in selective windows. With a worsening macro being factored in now, carry realizations could be pushed out even further, keeping reported earnings uneven. However, delayed earnings does not equate to lost value, especially for long-term investors willing to sit through near-term cycles. Accrued carry is still looking good, and there is no issue visible in investment performance across strategies.

I maintain a Buy rating for Blackstone (probably higher conviction for the valuation-induced better entry point now). Investors will need to monitor realization momentum and NAPR trends to gauge whether the accrued earnings versus delayed realization thesis remains intact. Deployment arbitrage in current conditions and FRE growth should also continue to be important. If realizations slow down meaningfully while NAPR also stops growing, that would indicate a genuine slowdown in value creation or monetization. I do not expect that situation to arise given how the Q4 numbers show strong realizations whenever windows appear. As this continues to be supported and becomes more even, rerating, alongside FRE increments, should push Blackstone significantly higher from current levels.

data.title: HYT: Improved Valuation, But Bonds Likely To Remain Under Pressure
data.description:
The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund (HYT) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a high level of current income. The fund does reasonably well at this task, as it boasts an 11.02% current yield at today’s share price, which it obtains through investments made in a portfolio of junk bonds. While this yield seems likely to attract many investors, the fact that the fund earns its income from bonds could be problematic. Over the past several weeks, bond yields have been rising, which has weighed upon the share prices of just about any bond fund. Furthermore, there could be reasons to doubt that bonds in general will be able to deliver positive real returns going forward, let alone reasonable returns. While junk bonds will almost certainly be better than U.S. Treasuries in this respect, they also have much higher default risk, and it is very possible that the real returns that they deliver will not adequately compensate investors for the risks that they are assuming. With that said, it might still be possible for an actively managed bond fund to deliver real returns through trading bonds, as my concerns are mostly centered around a buy-and-hold strategy in which an investor purchases a bond when it is first issued and holds it through maturity. There are some actively managed bond funds that employ such a buy-and-hold strategy, though, so we should pay close attention to the composition and strategy of any fund before purchasing it. Let us take a look at the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund and see if it could be a reasonable holding today or if the income that it provides is not worth the risk.

About the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund

The fund’s website describes the fund thusly:

The Trust seeks to achieve its objectives by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its assets in domestic and foreign high yield securities, including high yield bonds, corporate loans, convertible debt securities, and preferred securities which are below investment grade quality.

This description is much more expansive than just junk bonds. After all, it explicitly states that the fund’s portfolio might include things such as corporate loans and convertible debt. These securities are completely different animals than junk bonds. For example, corporate loans are almost always floating-rate securities. Unlike bonds, the price of a corporate loan does not really change much in response to macroeconomic events or interest rate movements. As we can see here, the iShares Floating Rate Bond ETF, which tracks a portfolio of investment-grade corporate loans, has been almost flat for nearly all of the past ten years:

With these securities, it is the coupon payment that changes with interest rate movements and not the price of the security. Furthermore, the amount of money that the coupon pays out usually changes based on short-term interest rates that are easier for central banks to control, rather than long-term interest rates that are typically market-based. As such, it is not generally a good idea to hold corporate loans whenever the Federal Reserve (or whatever central bank manages the currency that the bond is denominated in) is in the process of reducing interest rates. This is because the amount of money that the bond pays its investors will be declining, and it will not deliver price appreciation that might offset the reduction in income.

Convertible bonds also exhibit very different market performance than junk bonds. As the name suggests, these bonds can be converted into common equity securities of the issuing company under certain circumstances. Due to this feature, these bonds will frequently rise in price whenever the common stock of the issuing company rises. This is due to the fact that the conversion feature becomes more valuable as the price of the company’s stock rises. This makes sense because the convertible bond can be converted into common stock at a fixed ratio regardless of the common stock price. In some ways, it functions much like a call option with certain bond traits (such as coupon payments). As many readers likely know, call option prices also rise whenever the common stock that the call option is written against rises. This results in convertible bonds performing very differently from most bonds in the market. For example, this chart shows the performance of the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), which tracks the price of an index of convertible bonds, compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and both investment-grade bonds (AGG) and junk bonds (JNK) over the past ten years:

As we can clearly see, the convertible bond index delivered a performance that was a bit worse than common stocks but far better than either of the two bond indices. This is ordinarily what we would expect from such securities. We should also note that these are the only types of bonds that I would recommend for an investor who wants to buy the bond and hold it until maturity, due to the fact that their common equity-linked upside should do a better job of delivering positive real returns over the long term than will ordinary bonds.

Finally, the website claims that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund will occasionally invest in preferred stocks. This is also very surprising for a junk bond fund due to the fact that preferred securities are technically considered a form of equity and not debt. Admittedly, preferred securities do function similarly to bonds in some respects due to the fact that preferred securities almost always pay a dividend that is either fixed or variable, depending on some interest rate benchmark. Preferred stock dividends are not tied to the performance of the issuing company as common stock dividends are. That is similar to the way a bond or other debt security works. However, if a company fails to pay a preferred stock dividend, then it is not considered to be a default on a debt obligation. Furthermore, most preferred stocks do not have maturity dates, and the only way for investors to get their money back is by selling the preferred security. This is more similar to the way common equities function, not bonds.

As such, it is somewhat surprising that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund claims to be able to invest in these alternative security types, as including them in the portfolio will change the performance of the fund, perhaps drastically. However, the fund’s annual report states that its exposure to these alternative types of securities was somewhat limited as of December 31, 2025. This document states that HYT had the following asset allocation as of December 31, 2025:

Security Type

% of Net Assets

Asset-Backed Securities

0.2%

Common Stocks

0.8%

Corporate Bonds

108.5%

Fixed-Rate Loan Interests

1.4%

Floating Rate Loan Interests

8.5%

Investment Companies

1.1%

Preferred Securities

6.0%

Money Market Funds

0.1%

We can see that the bulk of the fund’s assets are invested in traditional high-yield debt. As of December 31, 2025, the fund held $1,707,518,403 worth of junk bonds, which was 85.75% of its total assets of $1,991,231,307. However, the fund’s actual exposure to junk bonds is slightly above this due to the composition of the “Investment Companies” category. This position is entirely invested in the iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY), which is a junk bond exchange-traded index fund that tracks the ICE Bank of America Merrill Lynch US High Yield Constrained Index:

As such, we can consider this to be junk bond exposure in addition to the fund’s direct holdings of this security type. As such, the presence of this exchange-traded index fund brings the fund’s junk bond exposure up to $1,725,772,922, or 86.67% of the fund’s total assets.

In a recent article, I stated that it is not unusual to see some exposure to common stocks in the portfolio of a bond fund. This is due to the fact that some bond issuers default prior to making the final payment at maturity. According to Legal Clarity, going back to 1981, bond issuers had the following ten-year cumulative default rates:

Bond Rating

Ten-Year Cumulative Default Rate

AAA

0.81%

AA

0.96%

A

1.57%

BBB

3.80%

BB

12.90%

B

24.20%

CCC/C

55.70%

A junk bond is anything rated BB or lower. As we can clearly see in the table, this level is when the default rates start to increase dramatically. A jump in the ten-year cumulative default rate from 3.80% to 12.90% between BBB (the lowest investment-grade rating) and BB (the highest speculative-grade rating) is a pretty large change that clearly shows how much higher the risk of a junk bond defaulting is when compared to investment-grade bonds.

As we can see here, nearly all of the portfolio of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is invested in junk bonds:

As this chart shows, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund only has 5.54% of its total assets invested in investment-grade bonds. All of the rest of the bond portfolio is invested in junk bonds. While the fund does have 3.45% of its assets invested in unrated securities, these are usually issued by entities that would have speculative-grade ratings if the issuer would have gotten the bond issue rated. After all, there is a significant interest savings with investment-grade bonds, so if the issuer was capable of getting such a rating, then it probably would have gone through the time and expense to have its securities rated.

What this all means is that many of the securities that are held by the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund have a pretty high chance of defaulting at some point in their lives. This is especially true for the CCC-rated and D-rated securities that comprise 14.83% of the fund’s portfolio. As these securities have historically had a greater than 50% ten-year cumulative default rate, the fact that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund has such a high exposure to them may be somewhat concerning to more risk-averse investors. Fortunately, there are two ways through which the fund attempts to limit its losses in the event of a default.

  1. The fund’s portfolio contains 1,211 positions. This limits the proportion of the fund’s total assets that is invested in any individual position, which limits its losses in the event of a default. After all, if a bond defaults, the fund’s losses will generally be limited only to the money that it had invested in that single position.
  2. As an actively managed fund, HYT can sell bonds if the issuing company is deteriorating. This is not an index fund that simply buys and holds bonds. We can see that the fund does frequently trade bonds in the fact that it had a 54% turnover rate during the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. While selling off bonds of a financially deteriorating company may not prevent the fund from taking any losses at all, it might be able to limit the losses that it takes by selling the bonds before the default actually occurs.

With respect to the second point, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund has historically had a turnover ratio that is high enough to suggest frequent buying and selling of bonds:

FY 2025

FY 2024

FY 2023

FY 2022

FY 2021

Portfolio Turnover Ratio

54%

73%

62%

45%

54%

(All figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31 of the respective year.)

These figures are all high enough to suggest that the fund is actively trading bonds rather than simply buying and holding them until maturity. This is not a bad strategy. After all, the only net investment return that an investor who purchases a bond and holds it until maturity will receive are the coupon payments made by the bond. It is possible to realize higher total returns by actively trading bonds and making money from interest rate movements, and indeed, in a situation where real interest rates are negative or negligible due to inflation, trading bonds can be the only way to earn positive returns in real terms. As regular readers likely know, I suspect that the United States, along with many other developed countries, may be about to enter a period of fiscal dominance in which central banks deliberately hold interest rates down in order to inflate away the national debts of their respective governments. That is a very bad situation for bondholders in general, but a fund that engages in bond trading might be able to earn positive real returns if the trading strategy is good enough.

The common stocks in the portfolio primarily come from bonds that defaulted while the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund was holding them in its portfolio. Normally, a company will enter bankruptcy protection prior to a default, and if it does not, then it will be forced into bankruptcy protection when the default occurs. In a Chapter 11 bankruptcy (most corporate bankruptcies are Chapter 11), the company restructures its finances, and this can involve bondholders receiving common equity of the company. This is why most bond funds that have been around for a while include some common stocks in their portfolios. The funds never purchased the common stocks directly, but they ended up getting them through a restructuring process.

If we look at the common stocks that are included in HYT’s portfolio, we get confirmation that it obtained most of the common equity securities through restructuring processes. Here are two of the common stocks in the portfolio:

Here are the rest of them:

One of these securities (Quorum Restructuring Equity) identifies itself as being the result of a restructuring proceeding. VICI Properties (VICI) was created via a restructuring of Caesars Entertainment (CZR), in which Caesars’ creditors agreed to split the company into two parts, one of which was a real estate investment trust named VICI Properties. ADLER Group entered into bankruptcy protection in 2023, and bondholders approved a recapitalization plan in 2024. Maxeon Solar (MAXN) has undergone multiple restructuring rounds over the past several quarters and applied for Singapore’s equivalent to U.S. bankruptcy laws a few days ago. Altice France filed for France’s equivalent to U.S. bankruptcy protection last year and Chapter 15 bankruptcy protection in the U.S. around the same time. Altice France’s common equity is also pretty commonly seen in many bond funds, as its debt securities were pretty popular holdings among bond managers.

The exceptions to the fund obtaining most of its equity holdings through bankruptcy proceedings may be Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). Neither one of these companies has ever gone into bankruptcy protection, nor has either of them ever defaulted on its debt. The fund may have purchased the common equities of these two companies due to their very high yields:

Company Name

Distribution Yield

Energy Transfer

7.08%

Enterprise Products Partners

5.86%

These yields are comparable to or better than most investment-grade bonds, and Energy Transfer’s yield is even higher than some junk bonds. As such, buying them does fit with the fund’s goal of earning an income from its portfolio, even though these securities are not fixed-income products. They also only account for a relatively small proportion of the fund’s portfolio, so unfortunately, any capital gains that they deliver going forward will not really benefit the fund by very much.

The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund Versus Exchange-Traded Index Funds

As of the time of writing, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund boasts an 11.02% distribution yield. This is substantially higher than the yields that any of the domestic or global exchange-traded bond index funds possess. We can see that in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index

3.94%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index

6.66%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.18%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF (BNDX)

4.47%

JPMorgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

5.15%

For quite some time now, the highest-yielding of the major bond indices has been the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks an index of speculative-grade bonds issued by American corporations. This is not the only junk bond index around, however, as the ICE Bank of America US High Yield Constrained Index, which is tracked by the iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF, claims to offer a broader exposure than other junk bond indices. That particular ETF yields 6.93%, so its yield is a bit higher than that of JNK’s index, but it is still lower than the yield of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund.

The current yield of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is also higher than that of most individual junk bonds. According to the Federal Reserve, the current yield of an index of B-rated junk bonds is 7.41%:

As we can see, the highest yield that B-rated junk bonds have had at any time in the past year was 8.71%. This was set on April 7, 2025, which was a few days after the so-called “Liberation Day tariffs” were announced and numerous market participants were expecting the tariffs to plunge the American economy into a recession. Defaults tend to increase during recessions, particularly defaults on junk bonds, so it makes sense that the yields of these securities would rise sharply whenever investors expect a recession in the near future. After all, the high yields on junk bonds are intended to be a way to compensate investors for the extra default risk carried by junk bonds as opposed to U.S. Treasury securities.

In order to find junk bonds that have similar yields to the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund, we have to go down to the CCC-rated bonds and lower. The Federal Reserve states that the ICE Bank of America US Corporate C Index currently yields 13.79%:

This index consists of all of the bonds included in the ICE Bank of America US High Yield Master II Index that have ratings of CCC or lower. In other words, these are the junk bonds that are considered to have the highest risk of defaulting. The yield of this index is higher than the yield of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund, but we can see in the chart above that for a good portion of the past year, the bonds in this index had lower yields than they have right now. However, the lowest yield that these securities had over the past twelve months was 11.43%, which was set on August 28, 2025. That is still higher than HYT’s current yield. As we saw earlier, the portfolio of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund consists mostly of BB and B-rated junk bonds, not CCC or lower junk bonds. Thus, the bonds in the fund have a lower default risk, but investors in it do not have to sacrifice very much yield to get that lower default risk. This would seem to be a pretty reasonable proposition, and it is probably worth it to accept a lower yield in exchange for the lower default risk, especially for investors who do not wish to take on a lot of risk.

One thing that we should note is that three of the index funds shown above – both of the Vanguard funds and the EMB emerging markets fund – is that they all include exposure to bonds issued by non-American entities. BNDW is usually pretty close to a 50/50 split between American bonds and non-American ones (right now, it is 51.70% American and 48.30% non-American), while BNDX and EMB all consist entirely of bonds issued by non-American entities. The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is somewhat different, as its holdings mostly come from entities that are located within the United States:

As we can see here, 85.57% of the bonds in HYT’s portfolio were issued by entities that are based in the United States. This is not really a problem, as the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund does not claim to be a global fund. Indeed, it arguably makes sense right now given that American junk bond yields are among the highest in the developed world. For example, the Federal Reserve reports that the yield of an index of junk bonds issued by entities in the European Union is 5.99%, considerably lower than comparable American indices:

Thus, it makes sense for a junk bond fund that is focused on earning income for its investors to focus its portfolio on American junk bonds as opposed to foreign ones. After all, the coupon payments are the only net investment return that bonds deliver over their entire lifetime, so generally speaking, the higher a bond’s yield, the more income and the greater the total return that it provides will be.

In a recent article, I stated that investors who hold exposure to bonds denominated in foreign currencies could stand to profit going forward from a persistent decline in the value of the U.S. dollar. An especially good opportunity exists in emerging markets to profit in this manner. One of the easiest ways to profit from this opportunity is to purchase shares of funds that invest in bonds that are denominated in foreign currencies. Unfortunately, the American-centric nature of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund prevents it from being a good candidate for investors who wish to take advantage of this opportunity. As we can see here, 96.63% of the bonds in the fund’s portfolio are denominated in U.S. dollars:

This means that these bonds pay their coupons in nominal U.S. dollars, and they return U.S. dollars to their owners upon maturity. There is no adjustment for any changes in purchasing power that occur between the issuance and maturity date. Thus, if the U.S. dollar declines in terms of what it can actually purchase (one basic definition of inflation), then investors might end up losing purchasing power over the lifetime of the bond, depending on the rate of purchasing power decline.

This is my biggest concern with any bonds today. Over the past ten years, investment-grade bonds have delivered a negative total return in real terms. As we can see here, an investor who purchased the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund (VBMFX) ten years ago and reinvested all of the distributions that it paid out since that time into additional shares of the fund ended up receiving a -15.22% total real return after adjusting for inflation using the CPI-U:

Junk bonds did a bit better, admittedly, but even the JNK index fund only delivered an average total real return of 2.06% per year using the same assumptions:

This suggests that, should a period of higher inflation than was seen over the past ten years occur at some point in the future, it is very possible that even junk bonds will end up delivering negative returns in real terms. As we will see later in this article, that is a possibility.

Fortunately for the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund, its higher yield relative to the indices has positioned it to earn stronger returns than is the case for the indices. We can see this in the fact that this fund managed to deliver an average total real return of 4.39% over the past ten years:

As shown, this fund managed to beat both of the indices over the trailing ten-year period. One of the biggest reasons for this is that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Bond has a higher yield than either of the two indices. As such, it benefits from stronger compounding over time, and this helps to offset the loss of purchasing power due to inflation. In addition, the fact that the fund has the ability to trade bonds and actually earn profits through that manner rather than relying solely on the coupons to earn a return provides it with superior returns over time, and that also helps it beat inflation.

With that said, investors in the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund would have only beaten inflation if they opted to reinvest the distributions that the fund paid out over the period. If we look solely at price return, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund delivered a 13.13% loss over the past ten years, which was worse than either of the two index funds delivered:

Obviously, there was not deflation over the past ten years. Thus, any investor who put their money into the fund and then went and spent all of the distributions that it paid out now has a position in the fund with less purchasing power than they had at the time that they made their investment. Many income investors are retirees or other individuals who might have difficulty replacing their principal, so this is not the most desirable situation. Fortunately, though, anyone who reinvested some of the distributions that HYT paid out over the period into additional shares of the fund could have preserved their purchasing power and still earned some income in the process.

The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund Versus Peer Closed-End Funds

The BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is able to boast a higher yield than most index funds for two reasons. The first is that the fund trades bonds and distributes the profits from this to its investors. Index funds typically do not do this. The second reason is that the fund borrows money from outside sources and uses this money to purchase bonds. The fund uses the coupon payments from the bonds that it purchases to pay off the interest on the borrowed money and keeps anything left over, which ultimately results in it having a higher yield on its net assets than it would without the leverage. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, HYT received $142,983,411 in interest on average net assets of $1,529,471,450, which works out to an effective yield of 9.35%. That is higher than the yield of any of the junk bond indices, so the fund is definitely getting a boost from its leverage.

As other closed-end funds can use the same strategies to boost their yields, we should expect them to have yields that are competitive with the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund. As we can see in this chart, that is indeed the case:

The median yield of this group of thirteen funds is the 10.42% possessed by the MFS Intermediate High Yield Fund. Thus, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund has a yield that is somewhat above the median level for its peer group. However, the fund’s yield is not significantly higher than the peer median level. This could suggest that the market has confidence that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund will be able to sustain its distribution going forward. However, the fund’s net asset value over the past three years is not encouraging, as we can see here:

This chart shows the net asset value of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund over the past three years. As we can see, the net asset value of HYT has declined by 2.54% over the period. This means that the fund paid out more in distributions than it earned in investment profits from its portfolio. On the surface, this suggests that the fund’s distribution is not sustainable. However, we also notice that nearly all of the net asset value decline has occurred since the start of this year. In fact, the fund’s net asset value per share was higher than its starting point as recently as mid-February. After that point, it began to decline relatively sharply.

The steep decline in the fund’s net asset value since February is hardly unique to this particular fund. As we can see here, both investment-grade and junk bond indices started declining right around the same time:

There are two major reasons for this. The first is that several bond market participants have been losing confidence that the Federal Reserve will be able to reduce interest rates this year. Recent inflation reports have not been encouraging. In fact, the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Index, which the Federal Reserve uses as its benchmark, has been getting worse since November of 2025:

In addition to this, the recent job reports have not been showing signs of a worsening outlook for employment. As you may recall, weakness in employment was the reason that the Federal Reserve gave for cutting interest rates at its final three meetings last year. The fact that more recent reports are no longer showing this weakness (in fact, the unemployment rate has declined since November) makes it less likely that the Federal Reserve will use this rationale to cut interest rates further. Finally, U.S. President Trump announced that Kevin Warsh is his choice to replace Jerome Powell as the Chairman of the Federal Open Market Committee that sets interest rate policy for the United States. Kevin Warsh is considered to be an inflation hawk by many bond market participants, although I personally doubt that President Trump (who has repeatedly demanded lower interest rates) would have chosen him if Mr. Warsh were not in agreement with President Trump’s demands. These factors combined have made the market less confident that further interest rate cuts will be coming, and as such, it has been changing its outlook for bonds to be “higher for longer.”

The outbreak of hostilities between the U.S., Israel, and Iran has also proven to be a negative event for bond prices. This is mostly due to the Iranians throttling ocean traffic moving through the Strait of Hormuz, an event that drove up oil prices all around the world. Oil is essential for transporting nearly every product that consumers purchase, and most consumers purchase gasoline as fuel for their own transportation needs. As such, whenever the price of oil rises, it can cause the prices of the goods that consumers purchase to rise. This could make the inflation problem even worse going forward, but it is worth noting that it is questionable whether or not monetary policy can do anything to stop prices from rising when the cause is an oil shortage. Nevertheless, the market still believes that the spike in oil prices due to Iran controlling traffic through the Strait of Hormuz reduces the possibility that the Federal Reserve will ease monetary policy this year.

The future outlook for bonds depends heavily on how long Iran is able to control traffic moving through the Strait of Hormuz and the Federal Reserve’s future actions. If the Federal Reserve hikes, which I doubt, then we could see further deterioration in bond prices and further declines in the net asset value of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund. Over the long term, however, I still suspect that inflation will be a permanent fixture in our lives, as the Federal Reserve may have to monetize the U.S. fiscal deficits, which are likely to continue to increase rather than decrease going forward. This will weigh on the ability of any bonds to provide positive real returns going forward, but the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund will probably be able to do a better job of delivering positive real returns to investors than any of the indices will.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund was on May 7, 2025. As such, about eleven months have passed since we last looked at this fund. While the Federal Reserve has cut the benchmark federal funds target rate by 75 basis points, bonds as an asset class have not performed particularly well. One reason for this, and perhaps the most important reason, is that investors in bonds are worried about the ability of bonds to actually deliver positive real returns and act as a store of wealth going forward. As we have already discussed, this is by far my biggest concern about the asset class as well.

The shares of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund are down 10.37% since the last time that we looked at this fund:

As is clearly visible, this is a substantially worse performance than either investment-grade bonds or junk bonds delivered over the same period. It is also much worse than the fund’s portfolio actually performed. As we can see here, the fund’s net asset value per share has only declined by 2.75% since that time:

Thus, the fund’s massive share price decline is somewhat misleading, as the fund itself did not perform nearly as poorly as the share price suggests. Ultimately, what happened here is that the fund’s premium evaporated. As you may recall, in my last article on HYT, I showed that the fund’s shares were trading at a price that was well above the value of the assets actually underlying each share and that it had a price that was quite a bit higher than its peers. Therefore, it was overpriced and poised for a fall at the time. The fund’s disappointing share price performance is not surprising in this light.

In terms of total return, investors in the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund did much better than the share price suggests, but the fund still underperformed both bond index funds:

The fact that the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund was the only one of the three assets shown to deliver its holders a loss over the past eleven months could be rather discouraging to nearly any investor. However, it has outperformed both index funds over the long term, at least for an investor who reinvested the distributions.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is to provide its shareholders with a very high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0779 per share ($0.9348 per share annually). This gives the fund an 11.02% yield at the current share price.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025. I provided a link to this report earlier in this article. As this is a newer report than the one that we had available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, let us have a look at it to get an update on the fund’s financial situation.

For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund reported net investment income of $113,304,184. That was not sufficient to cover the $149,497,018 that the fund paid out in distributions over the period. This is, to say the least, disappointing, as we would ordinarily want a fixed-income fund to fully cover its distributions with net investment income. After all, bonds are somewhat less reliable than common equities at delivering capital gains. They are primarily income vehicles.

Unfortunately, in this case, the fund was not able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on December 31, 2025, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund reported net realized gains of $8,659,576 along with net unrealized gains of $23,899,678. This gives the fund investment profits of $145,863,438:

This was not sufficient to cover the distributions that the fund paid out during the twelve-month period. This also represents the second year in a row that this fund failed to cover its payouts. This is rather disconcerting, and it may suggest that the fund will need to cut its distribution if the bond market does not improve in the near future. I will admit that I am not especially confident given the pressures that are being placed on it right now.

Valuation

Shares of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund are currently trading at a 7.93% discount on net asset value. This is a better price than the 6.18% discount that the fund had on average over the past month. It is also a much better price than the premium valuation that this fund traded at the last time that we discussed it.

The current valuation of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is quite reasonable when compared to its own historical pricing range. Over the past five years, shares of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 9.85% discount to a 3.44% premium on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 2.10% discount on net asset value, which obviously represents a smaller discount than the fund has today. Thus, the current price is reasonable for an entry point based on the fund’s historical average pricing.

Here is how the current price of the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund compares with that of its peers:

Expenses

The website for the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Bond Fund states that the fund has an expense ratio of 2.35%:

As is the case with many closed-end funds, however, the bulk of the fund’s expenses comes from the fact that it has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude these fees, the fund’s expense ratio drops down to 0.85%. That is not particularly expensive for an actively managed fund.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund is a very high-yielding closed-end junk bond fund that has become much more reasonably priced over the past few months. While the pricing here is reasonable, investors might still want to be cautious. The fund has failed to cover its distribution for two years straight, and there are signs of trouble in the bond market overall. After all, it appears that inflation may be getting worse, and inflation tends to result in bonds delivering disappointing returns in real terms. While this fund does look better than it did early last year, investors might still want to be cautious about jumping in too heavily.

data.title: 3 Big Stocks That Are Great Fits For An Option 'Dog Collar'
data.description:
I've written here and elsewhere about what I call the "Dog Collar" strategy. That's just my "pet name" for a typical option collar strategy, where a stock or ETF is surrounded by a covered call option and a protective put option.

I have identified 5 large cap U.S. stocks that meet my criteria, and I'll show some examples of recent setups below. First a few review points on what this is and isn't.

If you go into your favorite AI chat (I like Google's Gemini) and simply type "Isbitts Dog Collar,"

It turns out that an "Isbitts Dog Collar" isn't something your pet wears—it’s actually a specific investment strategy used in the stock market. The term was coined by Rob Isbitts, a veteran investment strategist.

Isbitts specifically uses the term "Dog Collar" to describe applying this strategy to stocks or ETFs that are currently "in the doghouse"—meaning they have recently sold off or are underperforming, but the investor still wants to hold them while limiting further risk.

Key Components:

  • The Target: A stock or ETF that has been beaten down (the "dog").

  • The Goal: To stay invested in a volatile asset while strictly defining the "worst-case scenario."

  • The Setup:

    1. Own the underlying shares.

    2. Buy a Put Option: Sets a floor for how much you can lose.

    3. Sell a Call Option: Generates income to offset the cost of the put, but limits how much profit you can make if the stock rockets back up.

That's all based on what I've written in the past about the dog collar concept. Frankly, I was pretty into it early last year. But then the markets made it very difficult to continue.

Why? Because for the past 6 months, the stock market has been a tale of quick pops that have not been sustainable. That's not a good environment for Dog Collars, or option collars in general.

Key features and process notes on Dog Collars:

  1. Identify stocks that have 2 clear features: they are not just down a lot (25-50% from their highs, typically). They are showing some signs of bottoming. If not now, perhaps within 3 months' time.
  2. To me, this is more about technical analysis than anything else. You see, I'm a decades-long, technicals-focused investor.
  3. As such, if I see a stock that has really been hammered, but showing early signs of nearing that moment where it gets "re-discovered," I get to be MUCH more aggressive than my typical risk-management-focused process ever allows. I will "take big shots with small amounts of money," I but I won't do that with large amounts of capital...unless I can collar it!
  4. The Dog Collar allows me to, financially speaking, put that dog of a stock on a leash. The leash is the protective put option.
  5. The covered call option is the other part of the collar, and covered call writing has been all the rage for income-driven investors. I am as big a detractor of that approach as anyone you will find, since my own research here has shown that the ultimate outcome for covered call ETFs is highly correlated to the underlying stock or index ETF it tracks. You are just getting total return in different package. And to me, that's not investing, it is deluding the investor.
  6. That's why the Dog Collar's most important feature is NOT the covered call options. It is the put options I purchase. THAT is where I can get more adventurous, and look financial danger right in the eye. I'll show you in the examples below.
  7. In a market where stocks gyrate up and down within a tight range, the Dog Collar protects, but it loses its bite, if you will. There is often a cost to put it on, and I accept that cost in exchange for capping my worst-case loss up front, when I put on the collar trade. But if the stock or ETF doesn't take off to the upside with 1-2 months of putting that collar on, that defeats the purpose. Unless I want to go out very far on the calendar, with an expiration date a year or longer. That introduces other issues, such as if the stock rockets higher, and is in danger of being called away. I can accept that, and buy or collar more stock, or simply "roll" the existing collar. But if I reach that point, I'm already detracting from the strong outcome I was aiming for.
  8. For stocks which are way down in price, qualifying as "Dogs," but which have been flat for a while, their volatility has likely dropped. In that situation, I may opt to initially leave out the covered call part of the Dog Collar initially, and just purchase the stock and a protective put option. I can always complete the collar later. This is a reminder of what I said above: the covered call part of the collar, to me, is just a way to reduce the overall up front cost of the trade. It is not the panacea some fund companies and investors seem to think it is. If the stock plummet further, your covered call income only offsets a small fraction of that loss.
  9. This transcends the whole "buy or sell rating" thing. You know from reading my work that I view that paradigm as breeding more misunderstanding of how investing should work, and fostering more of a gambling or gamification culture. I can't stop it, but I can point it out at every turn. The Dog Collar approach, and collaring in general, takes the edge off "being wrong" on the stock. Frankly, in a way a stop order cannot do. A put option is a contract, a stop order is a paper tiger.
  10. Lastly, I put a little wrinkle into many of my collars. It is known as a "forward collar." That's where the covered call option expiration date is further out in the future than the expiration date of the protective put option. I won't show any in the example trades below, since I do not want to throw too much at you in one article. I'll "play it straight" on those, with calls and puts expiring on the same date. The "normal" way.

The Forward Collar: rationale

Here's what the Forward Collar does for me:

  • Brings in more call option premium, making it easier to fund most or all of the cost of the puts.
  • Retains the "disaster protection" provided by the puts long enough for me to see if the stock is going to walk out of the doghouse, so to speak.
  • Places the focus where I want it to be: less on the option mechanics, and more on the chart pattern. These trades can always be unwound or amended along the way. This is the options market, super liquid in the case of stocks and ETFs I'd consider.

3 Dog Collars I like here

If you're wondering why there are 7 stocks listed there when the sub-header says "3 Dog Collars I like here," consider the other 4 as "honorable mention "for now. That, and to emphasize that I'd always rather apply a Dog Collar to a stock that shows strong profitability grades via Seeking Alpha's Quant Factor Grades. A high valuation grade is also helpful, as is a solid growth grade.

But remember, these stocks are down for a reason. So while I consider those grades beyond profitability (my quality bottom-line indicator), since I've taken the worst case scenario off the table (via the put option in the Dog Collar), I can take the chance that the stock breaks down further. Here are the 3 I picked from that list of "finalists" above.

MSFT

I'm going to show 5-year daily technical charts for these, since this is less about technical precision, and more about the stock market's habit of re-discovering blue chip names when the fall this hard. And with earnings season upon us, it is a good time to check these out.

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is just about 1/3 off its all-time high, which was set twice last year. That was around $550 a share, versus its $373 close last Thursday before the long weekend. That's also less than 15% above where it stood back in late 2021. Dog? Yes, it checks that box for me.

I typically start the collar search by striking the put around the current price. I find that in many cases, that results in the cost of the stock being essentially equal to the maximum loss. I rationalize this as "the put purchase is my main cost, and that gest me upside to the collar price."

Here's a summary of the collar setup for MSFT, one of hundreds you can find, and adjust the strike prices and expiration dates to your liking.

The bottom line with this one: $370-$470 range, 6.3% cost and upside/downside of 19.5% to 7.3%. More than 2.5:1.

UPS

The other 2 were chosen due to their strong valuation grades. United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) is a "double A" in that its profitability and factor grades are both A-rated. And the stock's price has been about cut in half since early 2022.

Here's the Dog Collar for UPS. It also expires this September. Strikes are $100-$125, cost is 10%, and that buys down some of the downside. As you see, 17.4% / 8.1% is the up/down tradeoff, so more than 2:1.

UNH

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) is the last one. Also a double A, as noted above.

Again, I would not touch these stocks on a pure chart basis. Not because they can't bottom here, but because there's still plenty of risk in them. But if I can define my line in the sand via the Dog Collar, I can consider it more readily.

Here's the collar example for UNH. $270-$360, again to September, and a cost of 7%. That's most of the downside risk as the max loss is 9.7%. The max gain is 22.7%, so again that's more than 2:1.

Action items

Are these Buy, Sell or Hold ratings? You know from my discussion above and in other articles, my answer to that is "that's an irrelevant question!"

When we buy a stock, we are taking on 100% of the risk to get 100% of the reward. My risk-managed approach wants to avoid doing that as much as possible. In my options work, I do it by giving myself a chance to get 100% of the upside, up to a certain price level. But defining my own downside, which will typically be 5-10% below the current stock price.

I see plenty of investors devoting so much time to calling tops and bottoms in stocks. To me, the markets function in a way that is far less reliable than before.

I don't want those dogs running loose. So I put a collar on them.

data.title: PIE: High Risk And Poor Long-Term Return Despite Recent Outperformance
data.description:
PIE Strategy

Invesco Dorsey Wright Emerging Markets Momentum ETF (PIE) was launched on 12/28/2007 and tracks the Dorsey Wright® Emerging Markets Technical Leaders Index. PIE has a portfolio of 102 stocks, a 12-month trailing yield of 2.14%, and an expense ratio of 0.90%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the underlying index is designed to identify emerging markets equities with high relative strength by comparing their price returns over a set period to that of a benchmark. The methodology starts from the largest 1,000 constituents by market capitalization of the Nasdaq Emerging Markets Index. A relative strength score is assigned to each eligible security, and approximately 100 securities with the highest scores are selected in the index. Constituents are weighted based on their scores, and the index is reconstituted quarterly. The portfolio turnover rate was 204% in the most recent fiscal year. I will use as a benchmark the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM).

Portfolio

About 57% of asset value is invested in large- and mega-cap companies, with a focus on Taiwan (48.4%) and notable exposure in China (15.5%). Other countries are below 9%. The aggregate weight of China and Taiwan (63.9%) points to high geopolitical risk. Compared to EEM, PIE overweights Taiwan, downplays China, and ignores South Korea and India.

The presence of Taiwan among emerging markets is debatable. South Korea has been considered a developed country by S&P since 2001, and Taiwan surpassed it in GDP per capita in 2025. Both countries are ahead of the E.U., the U.K., and Japan in GDP at purchasing power parity per capita.

The fund has a focus on technology (34.5%) and significant exposure in financials (21.1%) and industrials (14.8%). Compared to EEM, PIE mostly overweights industrials and greatly downplays consumer discretionary and communication services.

Risks related to individual companies are low to moderate. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 25.5% of assets, and the largest position weighs 3.1%.
