data.title: PetroChina: Positive On FY2025 Beat And Potential Catalysts
data.description:
I've initiated coverage on PetroChina (PCCYF) with a "Buy" rating.

Its recent full-year earnings surpassed expectations. Going forward, it could benefit from energy price appreciation and the improving profitability of its natural gas sales unit.

Corporate Overview

PYCCF is 3rd in the world's ranking of oil enterprises by turnover, as per its FY2025 call disclosures. I obtained the event transcript from S&P Capital IQ.

Domestic Energy Assets

The firm derived 57% of last year's "Operating Profit/OP" from the core upstream or "Oil, Gas & New Energy" division. The downstream/midstream "Natural Gas Sales/NGS", "Refining & Chemicals", and "Marketing" units contributed the remaining 26%, 10%, and 7% of OP, respectively.

It's mainly focused on China, which represented 89% of the previous year's "Oil & Gas" or "O&G" production volumes.

PYCCF Outperformed In 2025

The group's latest financials were revealed in a set of slides released last week.

It registered a '25 bottom line of CNY157.3B that implied a 2% beat (source: S&P Capital IQ). I'm impressed with the company's ability to deliver above-consensus numbers, despite an unfavorable market environment.

PYCCF's slides highlighted that "average brent crude oil futures" were down 15% for the prior year. During the same timeframe, its earnings contracted by a milder 5%.

My take is that the positive surprise was driven by leaner operations and product portfolio rebalancing.

PYCCF managed to lower its 2025 "Selling & General" or "SG&A" expenses by 6% to CNY56B. At the analyst briefing, it was explained that this was made possible by "stricter controls on non-productive spending."

Also, the FY25 per-unit extraction cost came in $0.01/bbl lower compared to the preceding year. This helped to partially negate a CNY50B topline hit from lower energy prices.

Separately, PYCCF's key downstream business was a difference-maker.

Its "Refining & Chemicals" segment OP rose 19% to CNY22B for FY2025 thanks to value-accretive mix shift. The volume changes for gasoline, diesel, kerosene, and specialty products in this period were -5.7%, -3.2%, +8.2%, and +5.6%, respectively. I believe the pivot towards high-margin jet fuel and other niche offerings paid off.

PYCCF didn't forget to reward its income-focused stockholders as well.

It kept the full-year dividend/share unchanged at CNY0.47, notwithstanding the net profit contraction. The implied payout ratio went up from FY24's 52% to FY25's 55%. The shares boast a decent trailing yield of 5.0%.

A Net Beneficiary Of Energy Crisis

SA News reported on April 5 that there's "mounting concern over access to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for global energy shipments."

In my opinion, the company's exposure to supply disruptions is manageable.

PYCCF indicated at the results meeting that only about one-tenth of its O&G is dependent on seaborne Middle East imports.

The firm's own estimates suggest that more than 50% of the energy it requires is sourced internally. The remaining supplies are either coming from unaffected regions or transported by land pipelines.

PetroChina's call commentary also emphasized its "diversified resource base and the flexibility to sell products domestically/internationally."

On the flip side, there's substantial upside associated with its future profits if the oil price uptrend is sustained.

As mentioned above, upstream operations generate the majority of the group's OP. "Brent could average $150 per barrel over the next six months" based on "Capital Economics" forecasts cited in SA analyst Lance Roberts' write-up.

Earlier on March 11 this year, UBS published a report (inaccessible to the public) titled "Earnings Sensitivity For China's Oil And Chemical Companies." The European bank's analysis indicates that PYCCF's net income can be 8%-11% higher for every +$10/bbl change in Brent.

NGS Business Has Financial Improvement Potential

The OP for PYCCF's "Natural Gas Sales" division went up 13% to CNY61B in FY25. I think the firm has successfully identified a key lever to enhance this unit's profitability.

NGS recorded CNY20B of losses pertaining to the imported gas procurement sub-segment four years ago. The deficit has shrunk meaningfully to less than CNY5B last year.

PYCCF has been progressively expanding its gas storage capacity over time to the current 17 "Billion Cubic Meters" or "BCM." This allowed it to have the opportunity to purchase and accumulate more low-priced gas during the off-season.

Looking ahead, NGS's profitability could become even better in my view.

Over half of China's natural gas consumption is supplied by PYCCF, according to the company's briefing disclosures. You would assume that its bargaining power has increased amidst the ongoing geopolitical turbulence. I see the group securing gas-related price hikes when existing agreements are renewed, typically in March/April.

I project that the OP growth for NGS will accelerate from the low-teens percent in FY2025 to 20+% for FY2026, considering the tailwinds outlined here.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

PYCCF's actual '26 margins might fall short of expectations due to execution risks. These can include a surge in SG&A investments or poor timing of imported gas purchases.

The company's shares might lose favor with the market if the U.S.-Iran conflict ends sooner than expected. Investors may choose to unwind their defensive energy hedges, such as PetroChina.

My Concluding Thoughts

I'm a bull on PYCCF.

The stock trades at 11.2x FY27 "Price-to-Free Cash Flow" or "P/FCF" now as per S&P Capital IQ.

The mean forward P/FCF for its domestic oil major peers CNOOC (CEOHF) and China Petroleum (SNPMF) is 13.5x. PYCCF's capital appreciation potential is +21% (13.5/11.2), justifying a "Buy."

I view higher-than-anticipated oil prices and NGS's margin expansion as re-rating triggers for its shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arm Holdings: The CPU Bottleneck Thesis Is Getting Harder To Ignore
data.description:
My appeal to Arm Holdings (ARM) is not based on smartphones or generic AI anymore but on the realization that agentic AI has been slowly pushing back the compute problem toward CPU architecture. To be clear, I'm not saying GPUs aren't important.

However, what we're seeing is a gradual evolution of the workload where GPU-centric training fades and more complicated workflows gain prevalence. As a result, we start seeing more hyperscaler design wins where the CPU component is significant enough for Intel's CEO to publicly acknowledge.

It's All About Value Capture Profile, Not AI Exposure

How would I approach the ARM discussion if you asked me about it? In my view, the wrong focus here is on whether ARM has some degree of exposure to AI. That's obvious. The right one is on whether AI is changing the way ARM captures value from its core business and customers.

In the old paradigm, ARM made money as an architectural enabler that participated in compute economics from the sidelines. There was always a ceiling to how much of the value it could extract in a particular end market, however big it was in aggregate. As a result, the company was well positioned to monetize compute, but it couldn't claim a meaningful slice of it directly.

The ceiling is potentially shifting. The royalty business is improving in terms of quality due to increasing Armv9 exposure and the growing importance of CSS. This matters far more than the absolute growth because it implies that an otherwise flat or modestly growing end market suddenly becomes much more lucrative if ARM extracts structurally more value from each device.

The more interesting development for me is that ARM is now trying to become a silicon enabler by launching the AGI CPU. I don't consider it a slam dunk, but neither do I underestimate its significance. If this works, ARM stops being just an architectural tollbooth in compute economics. Instead, it tries to become a full participant in it.

ARM’s Shift From Volume to Value Is Accelerating

Arm is silently pivoting from a volume-focused licensing business to a value-extraction platform, and it's more meaningful than 26% sales growth to $1.24 billion. The royalty revenue at $737 million (+27%) clearly became the major contributor, and in my view, this is structurally better due to the predictable nature and clear correlation to AI compute scaling. My focus, however, would be on Compute Subsystems (CSS), with 21 licenses already secured, as this means Arm can grab more of the silicon economics rather than the mere IP license fee.

Nonetheless, the big picture is about margin squeeze. The non-GAAP operating margin fell to 40.7% from 45%; meanwhile, free cash flow came under pressure, down to $169 million. No, I don't think this is negative because it's a conscious capital allocation toward R&D (+46%), as the company moves strongly towards AI, in the anticipation of reaching ~50% market share for hyperscalers.

Hyperscalers Are Proving My Point But Also Adding Some Uncertainty

Why do I believe so much in the thesis? For me, the biggest piece of evidence is that the world's most sophisticated buyers continue using ARM architectures. AWS with Graviton, Microsoft with Cobalt, Google with Axion, and Nvidia with Vera. While it's no surprise given what I already know about the industry, the fact that the market normalizes it should be viewed as a sign that ARM is now entrenched in the custom silicon strategy of hyperscalers.

This, however, is where I have some doubts. To me, the biggest advantage of ARM is also its biggest strategic threat. Hyperscalers who are validating ARM's thesis are also the ones who are capable of responding if ARM tries to compete with them on the product side. While I don't see any immediate channel conflict arising from that, as custom silicon roadmaps are sticky and lengthy, I doubt I can assume all of ARM's partners will react the same way.

For that reason, I pay more attention to what follows the product launch than to the actual product itself. In my view, the true test for ARM is whether it can sell chips without harming licensing relationships. If it can, the upside is very good indeed. Otherwise, the market may realize that transitioning from an architecture provider to a silicon merchant isn't that easy.

A Premium Backed by Momentum, Awaiting Proof

ARM is currently trading around $149, which represents roughly an 85x earnings multiple in fiscal year 2026, while the forward multiple is estimated to be around 70x for fiscal year 2027. At this price, the forward enterprise value/sales multiple is still around 30x, indicating that this valuation multiple already reflects a continuation of strong growth and multiple expansion. In my opinion, the interesting thing here is not only the premium valuation multiple itself but also how much depends on successful execution rather than waiting for time to pass.

A revenue estimate of roughly $5.9 billion in fiscal year 2027 would be reasonable, but there needs to be something else besides revenue growth to justify such an aggressive multiple. I am happy to pay the premium because of the improvement in the royalty mix and exposure to data centers, but the valuation requires a smooth combination of multiple drivers. It is not enough anymore to focus on the fact that ARM is growing.

However, the problem is that a significant portion of the story remains purely directional right now. I don't see anything fundamentally wrong with this valuation, as it assumes that ARM can continue generating growth based on the existing royalty model, keep improving it via Armv9 and CSS uplift, and begin generating incremental revenue streams through its direct silicon effort. I just don't see how this valuation can be justified easily.

Overall, I would describe ARM's valuation as highly expectation-based rather than inherently wrong. While the stock can sustain it in theory, it will have to be underpinned by growing credibility. If not, there might be a disconnect.

Risks to the Growth Thesis

As mentioned above, my main risk concern is that ARM faces an escalating challenge to grow its silicon business due to internal tensions between architecture and chip efforts. While I expect no immediate conflict to arise between these initiatives, slow growth of the AGI CPU might indicate that ARM's existing partners are wary of competing with it. If that turns out to be the case, I will likely have to reassess the value capture thesis.

Another reason to reconsider ARM's investment is a slowdown in data center royalty growth before this stream becomes dominant in the overall revenue mix. Currently, it supports the main thesis as well. However, if the growth slows down substantially while mobile is the main driver, the stock may become too speculative for me.

Finally, I would monitor the progress of CSS adoption, since any weakness there might suggest that the improvements in the core licensing model are temporary.

Bottom Line

As much as I think ARM has strategic potential, the shares won’t be easy to hold onto if they underperform. However, with the architecture prevailing and royalty machines starting to turn around and with the solid CPU bottleneck story, the stock is well positioned at these levels.

data.title: Tesla: Weak Start To 2026
data.description:
Last Thursday morning, we received first-quarter production and delivery figures from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). As the company looks to a future based on robotaxis and robots, the current business has not fared too well lately. Tesla announced very disappointing figures for Q1, especially when it came to the energy business, which could mean that 2026 analyst estimates see new lows in the coming weeks.

Previous Coverage of the Name

My most recent article on Tesla came back in late January, at which time the company had just reported its Q4 2025 results. Top and bottom line beats were reported, but the numbers didn't look as good as originally thought, and management did not give a concrete delivery forecast for 2026. Since that time to Friday's close, Tesla shares lost almost 18%, basically triple the 5.7% decline seen in the S&P 500 (SP500).

The Q1 Headline Figures

At the end of September 2025, the $7,500 U.S. EV credit ended, and some other countries, like Norway, saw certain incentives expire or wind down at the end of the year. Since Q4 started, Tesla has looked at increasing ways to drive demand, whether it be through lower-priced variants of the Model 3/Y or sales incentives like discounts and interest rate deals. As a reminder, last year's Q1 saw deliveries of about 336,700 vehicles, significantly constrained due to the refresh of the Model Y. In the graphic below, you can see what Tesla reported for this year's January to March period.

This was quite disappointing on both the vehicle and energy product fronts. A week ago, Tesla investor relations sent out their street-compiled estimates, calling for deliveries of more than 365,000 vehicles. Tesla also announced on Thursday that it deployed 8.8 GWh of energy storage products in Q1, substantially below the more than 14 GWh that the street was looking for.

In just the couple of days since Tesla detailed these Q1 figures, estimates have started to trickle down. The average revenue estimate has gone from $23.06 billion to $22.93 billion, which currently represents more than 18.5% growth from last year's constrained period. On the bottom line, analysts are looking for non-GAAP EPS of $0.39, down a penny in recent days, but that would be more than 46% growth over Q1 2025.

What to Watch for Next

It will be a few weeks until Tesla reports its full set of Q1 results on April 22nd. I'll be watching to see how much the discounts and lower-priced variants impacted revenues and margins. Also, credit sales have seemingly been winding down, and they have long been a key source of profits. Tesla management also expected to ramp up its spending as it looks to launch its robotaxi network and other artificial intelligence efforts. As the chart below shows, full-year street estimates for the adjusted bottom line have been dropping for a while.

The company is likely going to burn through cash this year, but it did have a very strong balance sheet to start 2026. If operating cash flows decline a bit this year, it will be interesting to see if Tesla decides to tap either the equity or debt markets. The end of March balance sheet will also have a slightly different look thanks to the investment in CEO Elon Musk's other company, xAI (X.AI), that was made a few months ago.

During this quarter, investors are expecting major updates regarding the Semi, Optimus robot, and second-generation Roadster. Tesla has also sunset production of the Model S and X. It will also be interesting to see if Tesla expands its driver-assisted taxi network to any new cities and whether any other cities get completely driverless vehicles. I will also be watching to see if Tesla investors move any of their money out of the stock once SpaceX (SPACE) goes public, which is expected to occur in June.

A Look at the Valuation Picture

When you look at Tesla, the stock itself looks extremely expensive for an automotive company. The stock finished last week at nearly 175 times its expected adjusted earnings for 2026. As a comparison, the big two traditional U.S. automakers' average numbers are in the single digits times their respective expected earnings. Even if you factor in Tesla as a part-tech company, the valuation is still many times that of the major U.S. tech giants, which mostly currently go for 20 to 30 times their respective annual earnings.

In a different light, in the chart below, you can see how Tesla matches up on a price-to-expected sales basis for 2026 vs. other EV names: Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), and VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY), BYD Company Limited (BYDDF), and NIO Inc. (NIO). All valuations are as of Friday's close.

The average of the seven comparison names was 1.36 times their expected revenues for next year. A number of these other companies are projected to see much higher revenue growth rates than Tesla in the short term. Of course, they are all working off much smaller base numbers. Additionally, Tesla is the only one of these names that has recently delivered meaningful profitability and positive free cash flow, outside of BYD at times, but the premium that shares trade for is massive.

As of Friday's close, the average price target on the Street was about $416, up about $5 since my previous article. That current average represents more than 15% upside from the day's finish, compared to 4% downside seen in my previous article. As you might expect with a stock that has long had such a bull/bear debate, the very wide price target range goes from $119 to $600. There are 48 analysts who have covered the name in the last three months; of them, 23 had some kind of Buy rating, with 17 Holds, and 8 sells.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

While Tesla showed decent year-over-year vehicle delivery growth over last year's severely impacted quarter, the numbers fell quite a bit short of street estimates. It appears that numerous promotions and discounts around the globe did little to spark sales, while the energy business was a major disappointment. As a result, Tesla shares tumbled more than 5.4% last Thursday, finishing the week almost 28% off its all-time high.

With this stock continuing to trade at a huge valuation regardless of whether you see it as a car or tech company, I'm continuing my Sell rating here today. The Q1 numbers announced Thursday were not good, to say the least, and that will only fuel demand questions even further moving forward. Investors were already on edge about Tesla's huge spending plans for this year, and the latest news will increase concerns about the company's near-term future.

data.title: Why Palantir's Best Days May Still Be Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Palantir (PLTR) closed Friday night at $148 around the same levels when I initially covered the stock last year and it’s down about 29% from the highs of $207. I am convinced that this is the best entry point in the stock since last summer. Not only is the business performing well in this pullback, it’s accelerating. They’ve been selected as a core software developer for the Golden Dome missile defense program. They’ve been expanding their partnership with Bain and they’re ready to report Q1 earnings on May 4 with a momentum story that just can’t be explained away by the bears, in my view. The price is down, but the story is bigger, so I am buying here.

The Thesis That Keeps Getting Stronger

I think Palantir has crossed the threshold that only a handful of software companies ever reach, the threshold at which both the government and commercial engines are spinning. The bear thesis for years has been that the commercial segment will never build enough speed to reach the government one. I believe the bear thesis is dead. In 2025, the United States commercial revenue growth was up 109% for the year and came in at $1.465 billion.

The United States government revenue was up 55% for the year and came in at $1.855 billion. They are growing in both segments, and I think it’s this unique attribute, the ability to be the only software name in the AI conversation with both flywheels spinning, that I think sets the company apart from every other name in the AI conversation.

The ontology layer that Palantir developed before anyone knew why it was important is exactly what the enterprise needs most today. The AI models are becoming commodities in an ever-increasing hurry. The hard part is no longer making those models; it's making those models connect to live data and all the control, audit, and security that regulated industries require. Palantir solved this problem years ago. My guess is that this advantage accelerates as time goes on, as the cost of models continues to go down and the difficulty of deploying them continues to go up.

Palantir has a $10 billion US Army framework agreement, a $448 million Navy ShipOS contract, and the Golden Dome win brings a brand new program that could dictate the entire spending trajectory of the entire defense industry for a decade. I firmly believe that the government pipeline alone justifies a significant portion of the current valuation before any new revenue from enterprise customers even begins.

The Valuation Question

I am going to be honest with you. PLTR is not cheap on a forward P/E basis. Anyone who tells you otherwise is not being realistic. However, I think that the question is not whether or not Palantir is cheap. Rather, it is whether or not the P/E they are at is in line with their growth rate. I think it is. Here is why.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives still has an Outperform rating on Palantir with a $230 price target. Analysts at UBS have increased their target price on Palantir shares to $200, citing an improved risk/reward profile following the correction in the stock from its recent highs in late 2025. Those are not $500 price targets from some analyst on the street but $200 or $230 price targets from analysts at institutions that have thoughtfully analyzed Palantir’s business model. The consensus target is $185. That means that at $148, Palantir is trading at a 20% discount to what Wall Street thinks that Palantir is worth in one year.

Again PLTR appears to be an expensive stock with its multiples being significantly higher than those of comparable firms like ORCL and NOW. However, upon deeper analysis, we see why it makes sense for the firm to carry this premium tag. While similar stocks grow steadily, PLTR experiences fast growth with their forward multiples being compressed as they scale up their profitability.

The real reason why we should not overlook the performance of this company is the quality of its execution and ability to achieve high growth without compromising its profit margins. Palantir's Rule of 40 is currently at ~127% and is expected to exceed 100% next year. This shows that PLTR belongs to a rare club of software companies that deliver growth and profits simultaneously.

Dilution and Capital

Share count has expanded modestly, roughly 4-5% over the last year, and is mainly related to employee equity plans. This is not ideal but is manageable. The SAR dilution event in 2024 is behind us, and the ongoing SBC is the price of retaining the best engineering talent in the business, which results in a 127% Rule of 40 score.

I would rather own a business that rewards its people in equity and compound at 127% over the last three years than one that tries to conserve its float and stagnates in place. Adjusted free cash flow in 2025 came in at $2.270 billion, 51% margin, full year 2025. The reality is that real cash flow is far and away outperforming dilution in terms of shares outstanding, and the math is on our side.

The Risks Assessment

Government contract risk is critical for Palantir, and it’s the one I worry about most. If federal government AI spending priorities shift or DOGE-era restructuring of the federal budget results in delayed bookings in Q1 or Q2 of 2026, near-term revenue could be disappointing against the guide. It won’t break the business but will be problematic in the near term for the stock price and could be severe given the beta of 2.1.

International commercial is the missing acceleration piece. US commercial is going through the roof. International commercial is increasing but has not yet replicated the bootcamp velocity we're seeing in the US. If we do not start to see some acceleration out of the international commercial side by mid-2026, we're going to have to adjust the revenue projections. This is the number I look at most closely every time the company reports earnings.

This stock has been between $66 and $207 the last year. This tells you everything about how volatile this name is to risk-off sentiment. If the equity markets start to sell off again, PLTR is going to get hurt more than the S&P. This is the cost of owning a high-beta compounder, and I'm more than happy to do so.

Conclusion

I'm going to be adding PLTR at $148 with conviction. This pullback from $207 is a sentiment event, not a fundamental event, in my opinion. I believe Palantir is one of the few software businesses in the world today where we're actually seeing increasing growth rates, increasing margins, and improving market positioning.

data.title: Shopify: Highly AI-Resistant, 'Buy' The Dip
data.description:
Many investors looking at the stock market in 2026 see a landscape fraught with risk: rising AI capabilities crowding out existing software names, escalating geopolitical tensions, and a challenging macroeconomy. But where others see risk, I see plenty of opportunity to buy shares in previously very expensive companies at a healthy discount.

Shopify (SHOP) is one of these attractive buying opportunities. The leading e-commerce software platform has fallen over 20% this year, despite continued strength in GMV and revenue growth, not to mention immense profitability and free cash flow that continues to expand.

I last wrote a "Buy" opinion on Shopify in November, when the stock was trading just shy of $170. Since then, shares of Shopify have fallen by nearly one-quarter along with other SaaS names, despite virtually no hiccups at all in the company's actual fundamental performance. The fall in share prices (and the ability to buy Shopify at a lower-risk entry point) only increases my conviction on this name, and I'm not hesitating to reiterate my "Buy" rating on this name.

Let's kick off the discussion with my take on the building "SaaSpocalypse" narrative. I think the most important thing to recognize is that the level of AI threat will vary widely from company to company. Certain software companies that have larger/enterprise-facing customers will be less prone to churn, simply because their internal systems are more complex, and to make wholesale changes is likely more costly than whatever they might save on subscription fees. I also think that application software that is less integrated into daily operations (such as those for smaller functional teams whose work is not fully integrated into other company activities, such as email marketing) is at higher risk of displacement.

When we consider Shopify, it's true that AI agents are making it much easier and cheaper to build professional, beautiful websites and storefronts. But there will still be a large segment of the customer base that will choose to use Shopify's AI tools to get a better guarantee of high professional quality rather than try to DIY their own sites. And regardless of how the storefront gets built, much of the backend services that power modern e-commerce (ranging from logistics and fulfillment to payments, as well as simple website hosting) will still require the kind of backend that Shopify provides.

In other words, despite what Shopify's share price might suggest, in my view, its business is relatively insulated from the AI threat. Meanwhile, we now have the opportunity to buy into the stock at a much better price.

At current share prices near $112, Shopify trades at a market cap of $154.021 billion. Netting off the $6.75 billion of cash on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $147.46 billion.

Shopify only guides one quarter out at a time. For Q1, the company is expecting to grow revenue at a low 30s pace, not decelerating at all from Q4.

For the full year FY26, Wall Street consensus is currently calling for the company to generate $14.65 billion in revenue, or 27% y/y growth (leaving room for some deceleration in the back half of FY26). If we assume FCF margins hold at 17% in FY26, FCF would be $2.54 billion (+27%/y). This positions Shopify's valuation multiples at:

  • 10.1x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 58x EV/FY26 FCF

Needless to say, Shopify is hardly a value stock. At the same time, this is a company that historically traded at high-teens multiples of revenue. Considering the consistent >30% growth pace here and Shopify's expansion of value-added services that are expanding the company's take rates against GMV, I continue to view this as a very opportune buying moment in a historically expensive stock.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Shopify's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Shopify's revenue grew 31% y/y to $3.67 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.59 billion (+28% y/y) by a three-point margin.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases Shopify's progression in GMV (gross merchandise value, or the sum total of all sales made through the Shopify platform). GMV in Q4 rose 31% y/y to $124 billion (barely decelerating at all from the previous two quarters, while still improving from low-20s growth throughout most of FY24), marking a very successful holiday quarter for the company. Shopify's strong GMV trends also defy the narrative of macro-driven, weak consumer spending.

Meanwhile, the company continues to bolster its array of products for merchants, with Shopify Payments continuing to be one of the principal levers for the company to achieve take rate expansion and revenue growth. The company processed $84 billion in payments in Q4, or 68% of total GMV (rising from 64% in Q4).

To me, the penetration rate of payments gives us yet more evidence of why Shopify is likely more AI-resistant than the markets are giving it credit for. E-commerce businesses have a range of payment options, ranging from Stripe and PayPal (PYPL) to Square/Block. And yet the overwhelming majority of merchants are choosing to keep payments in-house with Shopify, highlighting the benefits of an e-commerce software platform that can do it all. It's unlikely to imagine any merchants, especially on the SMB side, spending effort using AI to build up a storefront, payments processing, and logistics/fulfillment tools rather than choosing an established, high-quality provider like Shopify.

The company notes as well that Shopify is gaining more traction with B2B, not just B2C merchants. Per CEO Harley Finkelstein's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

2025 also saw serious traction in the B2B space. GMV from B2B merchants was up 84% in Q4 and 96% in 2025. We signed B2B giants like century old industrial manufacturer, Sonepar, and we grew our existing merchants like Away, who brought their B2B side of the business over to join the D2C side, now all powered by a single platform, Shopify.

And we're not just seeing growth from new merchants joining the platform. We're also helping our existing merchants continue to sell more. In 2025, GMV grew 29% year-over-year. And that's not just being driven by a few top performers. We saw huge traction across the entire merchant base. So that's the story of 2025, bigger, faster, more global and more durable than ever.

Shopify also continues to scale on the bottom line. Shopify's pro forma operating expenses as a percentage of revenue fell to 26%, down 1 point y/y.

Meanwhile, we note that GAAP operating margins rose to 17.2%, up 70 bps from 16.5% in the year-ago Q4. Shopify remains a solid "Rule of 40" software company, with no near-term risk of deceleration to lose that status.

Key Takeaways

Shopify remains a top-tier enterprise software company, and recent AI fears oversimplify how mission-critical Shopify is as a broad, multi-product e-commerce enabler for its clients. Shopify is trading at multi-year low valuation multiples near ~11x revenue. Take advantage of this dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: MP Materials: Buy Dip In Rare Earth Giant With Huge Upside
data.description:
I initiated coverage on MP Materials (MP), a leading rare earth miner and soon-to-be refiner in the US, in January 2024 when it was $17.96/share, which is still up 177% at current prices of $50/share.

After MP surged to $70 and then $100 in mid-2025, I published two other pieces that advised caution:

  • August 9 2025: MP Materials: Upside Exhausted Or Just Beginning? Published at $74.93 (price down 33% since then)
  • January 9 2026: MP Materials Is A Rare Earths Leader But Lacks Margin Of Safety At $60 Published at $62 (price down 20% since then)

My thesis in the previous two articles was that it was probably not a good idea to buy when the stock price was in a frenzy and it was worth waiting for the furor to wind down before deciding what to do with the stock. Since the August piece, MP has largely ranged or even fallen in the past 6 months.

However, with recent developments, I believe this warrants a revisit of the rare earth sector. In this article, I will deep dive into MP and upgrade this to buy again (and if the price goes down a lot in case of a stock market correction, it may even become a strong buy).

Mr. Market Myopic on this Sector

In my opinion, Mr. Market is rather myopic when it comes to this stock. Despite the immense value and the favorable underlying macro setting, Mr. Market consistently ignores this stock 90% of the time and goes into a frenzy when the headlines bark “rare earths” and fall back into a lull when rare earths fade from the 24 hour news cycle.

This can be seen in the wild gyrations in its stock price. MP traded at a low of $10/share in August 2024, and 1 year later, it hit a high of $100/share. Its value shifted 1000% in one year, but the fundamentals can’t really have changed that much in one year. It’s obvious to everyone who even glances at the headlines that MP is a leader in rare earths, the US is reliant on Chinese rare earths refining, and the US and China are having a bumpy relationship.

The fact that the stock market prices it differently by 10X in one year is more of a testament to the fact that Mr. Market has a very short attention span and focuses on what’s hot. If there’s sustained exciting news (like in AI), it keeps bidding prices higher, while if the news dies down, then the stock price sags. Witness how the rare earths sector seemed to be lackluster in the past 6 months, and MP declined from a high of $100/share to $49/share.

But I think Mr. Market’s myopic behavior provides an opportunity for long-term investors. The fundamentals are clear, the trends are clear, and while I don’t have a crystal ball and don’t know when MP will enter its next bull run (could be in a few days, weeks, months or even years), I have a high degree of confidence in this investment.

Macro background: An American supply chain independence play

The Strait of Hormuz chokepoint shows the importance of resource independence for those countries that can afford it and have the capabilities. While trade can be mutually beneficial, it is increasingly obvious that relying on other countries to keep the lights on is not a wise strategy in the current era. The US is less impacted by the Strait of Hormuz crisis precisely because it is self-sufficient in oil and natural gas, highlighting the importance of having a solid domestic production base.

It isn’t too far-fetched to draw a parallel with rare earths. These metals fuel the modern economy, and it makes sense that the US will want to diversify away from relying on Chinese refining and have some domestic refining capacity. Especially in the current age of turbulence, where the unthinkable apparently happens every other day.

When I was young, a potential Strait of Hormuz blockade was talked about here and there but was largely regarded as a hypothetical event. Only old eccentric geezers could believe this might actually happen! Well, now it’s happening, and resource independence matters for every major country. This is likely to enhance the determination of the US government and corporations to work with MP. The story gets better yet.

Lack of competition

It’s odd to me that in the modern world, trillions of dollars are committed to investing in AI data centers to produce chatbots, whereas only a few paltry billions of dollars are allocated to projects mining stuff that makes those AI data centers. Investors seem more excited to chase the vision of “AGI” (Artificial General Intelligence) or build physical models of the world rather than build productive stuff in the real world, but anyhow - this ends up being a net positive for MP. While there are other companies producing rare earths, there is barely any other major rare earth refiner slated for production in North America (the few that are under development are in Australia). This may enable MP to earn and sustain high margins once its refining capacities ramp up (more on that below).

AI complements rather than substitutes

AI’s impact on MP could be severalfold and could be overall beneficial. MP’s refining data will allow it to train AI models to help it refine more efficiently, thereby enhancing its moat. (There have already been plenty of applications of AI in metallurgy and I’m confident it will also have applications in rare earths refining). But AI would not substitute for MP – all the prompts in the world and the ability to write millions of lines of code cannot substitute for actually building and operating a refinery.

Valuation

In a recent interview, management indicated their long-term target would be about $650 million normalized EBITDA once they complete the ramp-up of refining capacity to the current 10,000 metric tons planned. But global demand for rare earth magnets in 2024 was 250k tons, so there’s plenty of room for MP to expand and secure a sizable market in the US, Europe, Japan, Australia, etc (Western-aligned countries). If, say, MP ramped up to 50k metric tons of rare earth magnets and maintained the same profitability, then it would correspond to $3.25 billion of normalized EBITDA (in all likelihood, profitability might be even higher from economies of scale). If MP ramped up to 100k metric tons of rare earth magnets, then this would correspond to $6.5 billion normalized EBITDA. Whichever metric you use, MP stands to achieve billions in normalized EBITDA if management achieves its rare earth refining goals. And given MP’s expertise and lack of real competition in the refining arena, I’d say its chances of achieving those goals are quite good.

Risks to bullish thesis

Now the reasons that I listed in my previous two articles that warned against being overly bullish on MP are still valid. Rare earths could stay out of the headlines as long as China and the US do not publicly spar over rare earths. And with no catalyst from the news, the myopic Mr. Market could continue to disregard the favorable fundamentals of MP. Also, MP will have a long way to go before it is profitable to the extent it can justify a $9 bn market cap.

Conclusion

I believe the recent 6-month lull in MP’s stock price is a good time to revisit buying this stock as an American supply chain independence play. Given its strong market position (undisputed leader in rare earth refining in the Western Hemisphere), solid operational history going back decades, the heightened international environment trending towards supply chain security and substantial potential to ramp up its refining capabilities, I believe the current $9bn market cap is worth a buy. However I would caution that I have no idea when my bullish forecast will come to fruition as this largely depends on when Mr. Market decides to pay attention to the stock (and this could be weeks, months or even years away).
